preamble.summary: A catalogue of the holy angels who watch: Uriel (over the world and Tartarus), Raphael (over human spirits), Raguel (who takes vengeance on the world of luminaries), Michael (over the best part of humanity and chaos), Saraqael (over spirits who sin), Gabriel (over paradise, serpents, and the cherubim), and Remiel (over those who rise).
preamble.remarkable: This is one of the earliest systematic lists of archangels with their specific domains of authority. It organizes the heavenly bureaucracy into a coherent administrative structure, reflecting the Second Temple period's increasing interest in angelic hierarchies.
preamble.friction: The number and names of archangels vary across traditions (cf. Tobit 12:15 mentions seven but names only Raphael). The specific domains are unique to 1 Enoch.
preamble.connections: Tobit 12:15 (seven angels who stand before God); Revelation 8:2 (seven angels with seven trumpets); Daniel 10:13, 21 (Michael as prince); Luke 1:19, 26 (Gabriel as messenger).

verses.text_reference: And these are the names of the holy angels who watch.
verses.rendering: These are the names of the holy angels who keep watch.
translator_notes: The faithful Watchers — those who did not fall — are now catalogued. The contrast with the fallen Watchers of chapter 6 is implicit.

verses.text_reference: Uriel, one of the holy angels, who is over the world and over Tartarus.
verses.rendering: Uriel — one of the holy angels — who is set over the world and over Tartarus.
translator_notes: Uriel ('light of God') oversees both the world and Tartarus (the place of punishment). The use of the Greek term Tartarus here connects to 2 Peter 2:4, which uses the same word for where God cast the sinning angels.

key_terms.note: A term borrowed from Greek cosmology for the deepest place of punishment. Used in 2 Peter 2:4 for the prison of fallen angels.

verses.text_reference: Raphael, one of the holy angels, who is over the spirits of men.
verses.rendering: Raphael — one of the holy angels — who is set over the spirits of humanity.
translator_notes: Raphael ('God heals') — his oversight of human spirits connects to his role as healer and his task of binding Azazel (chapter 10:4-6). In Tobit, he heals Tobit's blindness and delivers Sarah from a demon.

verses.text_reference: Raguel, one of the holy angels who takes vengeance on the world of the luminaries.
verses.rendering: Raguel — one of the holy angels — who executes judgment on the world of the luminaries.
translator_notes: Raguel ('friend of God') oversees the luminaries — the stars. His role in executing judgment on them connects to the imprisoned stars of 18:13-16.

verses.text_reference: Michael, one of the holy angels, to wit, he that is set over the best part of mankind and over chaos.
verses.rendering: Michael — one of the holy angels — who is set over the best part of humanity and over chaos.
translator_notes: Michael ('who is like God?') oversees both humanity's elect and cosmic chaos — a dual role as protector and cosmic enforcer. Daniel 12:1 calls him 'the great prince who protects your people.'

verses.text_reference: Saraqâêl, one of the holy angels, who is set over the spirits, who sin in the spirit.
verses.rendering: Saraqael — one of the holy angels — who is set over the spirits who sin in the spirit.
translator_notes: Saraqael oversees spiritual sins — transgressions committed at the spiritual level, perhaps including the Watchers' rebellion itself.

verses.text_reference: Gabriel, one of the holy angels, who is over Paradise and the serpents and the Cherubim.
verses.rendering: Gabriel — one of the holy angels — who is set over paradise, the serpents, and the cherubim.
translator_notes: Gabriel ('strength of God') oversees paradise, serpents, and cherubim — a domain that connects back to Genesis 3:24 (cherubim guarding Eden). His oversight of serpents may relate to the guardianship against the primordial serpent.

verses.text_reference: Remiel, one of the holy angels, whom God set over those who rise.
verses.rendering: Remiel — one of the holy angels — whom God set over those who rise.
translator_notes: 'Those who rise' likely refers to the resurrected dead. Remiel's role connects to the resurrection hope that becomes explicit in later sections of 1 Enoch and in Daniel 12:2.
